lines.text: Against Me!. 'Sink, Florida, Sink.' 2007. New Wave. Laura Jane Grace, James Bowman, Andrew Seward, Atom Willard. We're {{ELAPSED}} in and the night's collapsing like a bad roof in a hurricane.
Grace moved to Gainesville in '97. Built the whole band around 'the world is against me' — and you know what? She was right. The whole world WAS. The whole world IS.

They made this record on Sire. Major label. Hit 57 on the Billboard 200. Not because they sold out — because they *stayed true* and the world had to listen anyway. That's the punk move. That's the move that matters.
Grace came out as trans in 2012. Said she 'drop-kicked' the band in the face telling them. Most awkward hug ever. Then they *stayed*. They kept playing. That's solidarity. That's what real means.
'Sink, Florida, Sink' — it's about drowning where you're from. About watching your home collapse. I get it, Laura. We're all watching something sink tonight.
The Bouncing Souls. 'Ole.' 1999. Hopeless Romantic. The song that — I'm going to say it — the song that keeps people *alive*.
One of their guys was homeless. Street corner. Heard a song in a parked car and it gave him hope. So he wrote this. He wrote hope into the world because he *needed* it. That's what 'Ole' is.
Greg Attonito, Pete Steinkopf, Bryan Kienlen. New Brunswick, New Jersey. High school friends who decided to move to a college town and just — *build something*. No plan. Just belief.
They called their fans 'True Believers.' Not as a joke. As a *fact*. You were either in or you weren't. No fence-sitting. The Souls don't do fence-sitting.

'Ole' is the singalong. It's the moment at 04:00 in the morning when you realize you're not alone because forty thousand other people are singing it too, barricaded in their homes, listening to PRGE, refusing to give up.

tracks.title: A Better Place, A Better Time

lines.text: Hot Water Music. 'A Better Place, A Better Time.' 1997. Fuel for the Hate Game. Chuck Ragan, Chris Wollard, Jason Black, George Rebelo. All of them from Gainesville.

Named after a Bukowski short-story collection. Charles Bukowski. The man who wrote drinking into scripture. The man who understood that sometimes the only honest thing you can do is say it's all falling apart.

Black moved to Gainesville first. Said 'come here, there's something happening.' Three other guys left their hometowns and *believed him*. Just like that. Four guys decided to build something in a college town.

The dual vocals — Ragan and Wollard doing the call-and-response thing — it's like two people screaming *at* each other because they're the only ones who understand. It's the most beautiful argument ever recorded.

They broke up in '98 because touring was *destroying* them. Literal breakdown. Then they took three months apart and came back and realized the thing they built was worth *saving*. Reunion show, August 28, 1998. They almost didn't make it.
'A Better Place, A Better Time' — that's not optimism. That's *work*. That's the refusal to accept that this is all we get.

tracks.title: Sunday Morning
tracks.artist: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes

lines.text: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes. 'Sunday Morning.' 1996. Have a Ball. The album that covers 1960s and '70s pop like they're picking the songs *back up from the grave*.

Spike Slawson, C.J. Ramone — *Ramone*, by the way, not Joey but *related*, that's the lineage you're looking at — Joey Cape, Pinch, John Reis. Four bands on permanent hiatus just to cover songs for fun.
Fat Mike of NOFX got so caught up in their 'Wild World' cover he thought he was 'Fat Stevens, a guitar-toting Islamic Extremist.' The guy was *that committed* to the bit. That's the energy.

They played a kid's Bar Mitzvah. Thirty people. Got booed at PNC Park after a Pirates game and had the rest of their stadium gigs cancelled. Lost a fireworks show because they weren't *right enough*. And they kept playing.
'Sunday Morning' — take the softness of the original and punk it until it *bleeds*. Make the beauty violent. That's what they do. That's the Gimmes' whole *deal*.
The Lawrence Arms. 'Coming Home.' 2002. Apathy and Exhaustion. Brendan Kelly, Chris McCaughan, Neil Hennessy. Chicago.

The band's *name* — the Lawrence Arms apartment complex where Kelly and McCaughan lived. They got *evicted in the middle of the night* and named their whole existence after it. That's poetry. That's punk.

Fat Mike signed them to Fat Wreck Chords in 2001. But before that — before *any* of that — they recorded a full-length album, A Guided Tour of Chicago, and released it *before they'd even played a single show*. Belief first. Proof later.

Kelly said — and I'm going to quote him because it matters — 'We all have a lot of experiences, good ones and terrible ones, that are influenced by us being influenced by alcohol. That's what we write about.' That's the confession. That's the contract.

2002 Warped Tour. They got booted for calling out the organizers for selling bottled water for over five bucks. Booted from a *festival* for saying 'this is wrong.' That's not irony. That's integrity at cost.
'Coming Home' — it's about the place you can't go back to because it's already *gone*. But you come back anyway. You come home because that's where you belong, even if it's wreckage.
NOFX. 'The Decline.' 1999. Eighteen minutes. One single track. Fat Mike, Erik Sandin, Eric Melvin, El Hefe. The most *important* thing this band ever did.

Fat Mike started this band in LA in 1983 with two guys from Fairfax High School. Named it after a Boston hardcore band that didn't even have a *full album*. Borrowed the name from people who *didn't exist yet*. That's faith.
'The Decline' is eighteen minutes of *fiery and cynical social commentary*. No chorus. No reprieve. Just — *this is what we've done, this is what we are, this is what's coming*. Pure clarification.
Fat Mike's whole label, Fat Wreck Chords, was built because major labels didn't want them. They stayed independent. Stayed *true*. And somehow — *somehow* — Punk in Drublic went *gold*. Went gold.
They made videos and refused to send them to MTV because they didn't want to be part of the machine. In the '90s, when MTV still *mattered*, Fat Mike said no. I'm not doing that. Not for you.

2003, The War on Errorism. Anti-George-W-Bush album. They've *always* been political. But they got *more* political. More angry. More *necessary*. And now we're in 2169, {{ELAPSED}} into a Purge that Fat Mike predicted in slow motion.
'The Decline' — it's the final track. It's the one that plays at the end of everything because there *is no end*, just the endless catalog of ways we've failed. And we keep *playing anyway*.

tickerItems.text: Madison cumulative: 165 kills. 12 in the last hour. East Side holding at 34 YoY. Power outages widespread — backup systems active. Mendota shoreline clear as of 03:45.
Flashlight-dependent operations. All broadcast equipment on backup power. Signal stable. Next hour: pre-dawn surge anticipated. Stay tuned.
MorningCare Triage: 22-minute response time as of {{CLOCK}}. Pre-register your injuries. Post-Purge care begins at 07:15. Call now.
